title: Managing finisher pig growth and condition
subtitle: Monitoring weight gain and adjusting rations in the final weeks.
excerpt: Pigs in the finishing period (60 kg to slaughter weight) grow fastest and lay down fat most readily. Overfeeding produces excessive backfat and reduces killing-out yield; underfeeding slows growth and extends time to slaughter. Regular tape-weighing and daily observation give you the data to feed correctly and book the abattoir at the right moment.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, finishing pig nutrition guidance, ahdb.org.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs (2003). SAC Consulting Scotland, pig enterprise management factsheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: The proportion of the live animal's weight that becomes carcass weight after slaughter. In pigs this is typically 70 to 75%. A 90 kg live pig produces a carcass of around 63 to 68 kg. Excessive backfat, gut fill, and skin all reduce KO%.

content.text: A finisher pig growing well adds around 700 to 900 g of liveweight per day. That rate slows as the pig approaches slaughter weight and fat deposition increases relative to lean muscle. Recognising the transition and adjusting rations accordingly is the key management skill in this phase.

Weigh pigs every two weeks using a tape measure. Take two measurements: the heart girth (circumference immediately behind the front legs) and the length (from the poll to the tail base). The pig tape formula is: length × girth² ÷ 400 (all measurements in cm) = approximate weight in kg. A digital scale is more accurate but tape-weighing is reliable enough for scheduling the abattoir booking.

Run your hands along the spine and over the back of each pig weekly. A well-conditioned finisher has ribs that are felt with firm pressure but not visible; a thin film of fat over the backbone. Ribs clearly visible from two metres away indicates underfeeding. A pig that cannot be rump-scored (too much fat to feel the pin bones) is approaching the top of commercial backfat tolerance.

From 60 kg to approximately 80 kg, feed at 2.5 to 3% of bodyweight per day in compound finisher pellets (16% protein, adequate fibre). Above 80 kg, reduce to 2 to 2.5% to limit excess fat deposition without slowing lean growth significantly. Ad-lib feeding to heavy weights produces the fastest growth but the highest
cost. For a home-kill pig where flavour matters more than lean yield, slightly higher condition is acceptable.

Back-calculate the abattoir booking date from the target slaughter weight. If a pig is currently at 65 kg and growing at 700 g per day, it needs 25 kg of gain to reach 90 kg, approximately 36 days. Book the slot 5 to 7 days early to allow for variation, and arrange the eAML2 movement notification as soon as the slot is confirmed.
